events.content:
The Edgware Road. The old Roman road north out of London — still busy, still a working artery, though the coaching trade has thinned since the railways arrived. The inns that line it have adapted or haven't, and you can tell which is which by how they smell.

There is something transitional about this street. It has not entirely decided what it is now that the coaches run less. The Metropolitan Railway platform is below. The Shambles and the Concern both move through here without acknowledgement.

The Lamb and Flag has been a pub since before anyone living can remember and will be one after they are gone. Low ceiling, wide fireplace, the particular smell of a room that has absorbed decades of smoke and ale and conversation.

The clientele here is mixed in a way that the rest of the street is not — market men, road workers, the occasional professional slumming. It is neutral ground, which is why both factions use it.

tags: The passage is here. She does not yet know it exists.
events.content:
Under the road — a brick-lined passage, originally drainage, long since repurposed. It smells of the road above and something older below. Wide enough for one person moving quickly or two moving carefully.

Rook told her about it at rep 3. Not as instruction — as information. *In case you ever need to move without using the street.* She has used it twice. It has been useful both times.

A yard behind the coaching inn. Crates stacked against the wall, a drain in the centre, a single gas lamp that gives more shadow than light. Not overlooked from the street.

This is where things are exchanged that cannot be exchanged publicly. Both factions know it exists. Neither acknowledges the other's use of it.

tags: She has not been told about this arch yet.
events.content:
A railway arch under the Farringdon Street viaduct — one of many, most used for legitimate storage, this one less so. The brickwork here is original — from the 1860 construction, the mortar still showing trowel marks.

A crate. Heavier than it looks. She has moved it once before, on a different job for a different employer. She did not know then whose crate it was.

A wide stone fireplace, burning even in mild weather — the inn has always burned it, a matter of tradition or habit. Men sit near it to talk. The fire is loud enough to cover conversation without anyone having to acknowledge that is why they have chosen these seats.

On one specific evening, during Shambles job 3, two men are at the fireside seats who do not belong to the same world. One has a Smithfield accent. One arrived in a cab from the west. They are not looking at each other.

A wooden crate identical to the one from Callow job 4. The same iron banding, the same stencilled mark she does not recognise. Heavier than it should be for its size.

She was told to move it from this arch to the King's Cross luggage entrance via the luggage van. She has a pass. She has done this before. *Move* it when ready.

The passage opens onto a side street near the Paddington approach. From here she can reach the canal basin and the western stations without going above the main road.

Useful. Rook was right to tell her.

A large man behind the bar who has developed the particular talent of seeing everything and appearing to notice nothing. He has been running this pub for fifteen years. He knows exactly who uses the yard and exactly what they exchange there.

He says nothing about it because the people who use the yard are also the people who drink at his bar, and the bar is his livelihood.

A market trader at Smithfield who has been talking to people he shouldn't — specifically, a man who works for a newspaper, and two men whose affiliation Rook has not named but whose interest in Smithfield's internal arrangements is obvious.

The Shambles want him frightened, not hurt. The method is Ida's.

A man who arrived in a cab from the west — Paddington direction, the landlord will confirm for the price of a penny. He has a good coat and the careful stillness of someone who has been told to wait and is experienced at it.

He is the Concern's representative. He does not know that the man he is meeting at the fireside is Shambles. The Shambles man does not know he is Concern. They have been introduced through an intermediary who described both as independent businessmen.

They are not.

He sets a drink on the bar without being asked. Then he looks at Ida and removes it.

*Can I help you?* The question is not hostile but it is pointed. She is the wrong age and the wrong shape for this pub.

tags: What do you know about the yard?
events.content:
He looks at her for a moment — differently than the first time. Rook's name, or the name she has given, has reached him.

*You're one of his*, he says. Not a question. He refills something. *What do you need?*

*Shambles use it Tuesdays*, he says. *Someone else uses it other days. I don't ask who.*

He looks at her steadily. *A man in a good coat was here last Thursday. Came from the west. Met someone with a Smithfield accent.* He pauses. *Didn't look like business I recognised.*

*That's a penny's worth*, he says.

*No back room*, he says. *Yard. And I don't know what happens in it because I don't look.*

He goes back to the glass he is cleaning. The subject is closed.

He sells her an apple without looking up. His attention is on the gate — watching for someone or avoiding someone, hard to tell which.

He does not know yet that the Shambles is watching him.

He sees Ida and his expression changes — not recognition, but the specific unease of someone who has been waiting for consequences and is not sure if this is them.

He is ready to be frightened. The method is hers.

She places the Shambles token on the stall between them. She says nothing.

He looks at it. He looks at her. The colour goes out of his face in a way that has nothing to do with illness.

*Right*, he says. His voice is steady but his hands are not. *Right. I understand.*

He picks the token up and hands it back. He does not keep it. He does not want it on his person.

She says the name. Quietly, without emphasis, the way you say a name that does not need emphasis.

He goes very still. The customer he was serving waits. He seems to have forgotten the customer exists.

*Tell him*, he says finally, *that it won't happen again.*

*I will*, Ida says. She leaves. Behind her she can hear him breathing.

Ida stands at his stall and looks at him.

She does not buy anything. She does not say anything. She stands at his stall and looks at him the way a person looks when they are memorising something.

After forty seconds he says: *All right. All right.* He looks away first. *Tell Rook I understand.*

Ida walks away. The silence did the work.

He does not look at Ida. He is watching the door for the man he is meeting, who has not yet arrived.

His coat is too good for the Lamb and Flag. His hands are clean. He is from the west of the city — Paddington direction — and he is here to give something or receive something from someone he believes is independent.

He is wrong about that.

At rep 4, Rook pauses after the job debrief.

He takes something from his pocket — a key, iron, old. He looks at it for a moment before placing it on the table.

*This has been in my possession for six years*, he says. *I was given it by a man who worked the original tunnel dig. He said it opened something under King's Cross that shouldn't have been sealed.* He looks at it. *I've never found what it opens.*

He pushes it across the table. *You move through those stations better than anyone I know. If you find a door — you'll know.*

Old iron, heavier than its size warrants. The bow is plain, the bit unusually shaped — not a standard lock pattern. It has been carried for years; the surface is smooth where hands have held it.

Rook did not know what it opens. He has had it for six years. Someone gave it to him during the original Metropolitan Railway dig and told him it mattered.

She turns it over in her fingers on the train. Somewhere under King's Cross there is a door.

A worn coin, not currency — struck with a mark that has no official meaning but a very specific unofficial one. Everyone in the relevant parts of London knows what it means to see this placed on a surface in front of you.

Rook gave it to her at rep 2. *Show it when words would take too long*, he said.

A fragment of paper — half a page, torn rather than cut — found in the contested yard after the meeting. Someone dropped it, or discarded it, or it fell from a coat.

On it: a partial street name in Clerkenwell, a figure that might be a purchase price, and the words *Phase 2 clearance*. The handwriting is formal. Legal.

She has seen this street name before. The journalist mentioned it on the train.

Two men in the yard behind the Lamb and Flag. One with a Smithfield accent — Lower faction, she recognises the register now. One who arrived in a cab from the west, coat too good for this street.

They do not greet each other. They stand at the centre of the yard for six minutes. Something passes between them — a document or a small package, she cannot see clearly from the inn doorway. Then they leave by separate exits.

Neither faction knows the other was there. Both believe they were meeting an independent intermediary.

They were not meeting an intermediary. They were meeting each other, unknowingly, through the same broker.

Ida stands in the doorway for a long time after they leave.

The blackmail money ends at the Lamb and Flag — Shambles territory. The contested yard meeting connects a Shambles representative to someone from Paddington. The journalist is tracking compulsory purchases in Clerkenwell. Harwick's name appears in the Quill's false medical certifications and in the railway company's private ledger.

Three factions. One plan they are each executing without knowing the others are part of it.

Or — and this is the thought that arrives at Edgware Road and does not leave — someone is running all three of them simultaneously.

She does not know who. She is beginning to think she knows where to look.

After moving the crate, Rook has one more instruction: leave something in the contested yard behind the Lamb and Flag before midnight. The yard is at the back of the coaching inn on Edgware Road.

When Ida arrives, she is not alone. Two men are in the yard. Neither knows she is watching.

A market trader at Smithfield has been talking to people he shouldn't. The Shambles want him frightened, not hurt. Ida must make him understand that his conversations have been noticed. The method is hers.

During the constable surveillance, Ida noticed something else at the Lamb and Flag — a man from the west meeting a Shambles contact at the fireside. Hidden observation quest. Sets up the contested yard encounter.

tags: Into the passage under the road.
She does not yet know the passage is here.
North end — emerges near the canal basin.
She has not yet explored this end of the passage.
To the storage arch under the viaduct.
She has not been directed here yet.
Board the westbound train toward Paddington.
The guard holds out his hand.
